examples.title: SMTP Email Send Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode showing SMTP protocol interaction sequence
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SendEmail(sender, recipient, subject, body)
BEGIN
 // Establish TCP connection to SMTP server
 socket = ConnectToServer(smtp_server, port 587)
 
 // Wait for server greeting
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 IF response.code != 220 THEN
 RETURN ERROR "Connection failed"
 END IF
 
 // Send EHLO (Extended HELLO)
 SendCommand(socket, "EHLO " + client_domain)
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Start TLS if available
 IF response contains "STARTTLS" THEN
 SendCommand(socket, "STARTTLS")
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 // Re-establish connection with TLS
 socket = UpgradeToTLS(socket)
 SendCommand(socket, "EHLO " + client_domain)
 END IF
 
 // Authentication (if required)
 SendCommand(socket, "AUTH LOGIN")
 SendCommand(socket, Base64Encode(username))
 SendCommand(socket, Base64Encode(password))
 
 // Specify sender
 SendCommand(socket, "MAIL FROM:<" + sender + ">")
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Specify recipient
 SendCommand(socket, "RCPT TO:<" + recipient + ">")
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Send email data
 SendCommand(socket, "DATA")
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Construct and send full message
 message = "From: " + sender + "\r\n"
 message += "To: " + recipient + "\r\n"
 message += "Subject: " + subject + "\r\n"
 message += "Date: " + CurrentDateTime() + "\r\n"
 message += "MIME-Version: 1.0\r\n"
 message += "Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8\r\n"
 message += "\r\n"
 message += body + "\r\n"
 message += ".\r\n"
 
 SendCommand(socket, message)
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Quit session
 SendCommand(socket, "QUIT")
 CloseSocket(socket)
 
 RETURN SUCCESS
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the SMTP email sending process: establishing TCP connection, EHLO handshake with server capability detection, optional TLS upgrade for encryption, authentication, MAIL FROM and RCPT TO commands to specify envelope, DATA command to send message content with headers, and QUIT to close session.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per email - depends on network latency
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is email message size

examples.title: Email Header Parser
examples.description: Pseudocode for parsing email headers to extract key information
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ParseEmailHeaders(emailContent)
BEGIN
 headers = Dictionary()
 bodyStart = -1
 
 lines = Split(emailContent, "\r\n")
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO lines.length - 1 DO
 line = lines[i]
 
 // Empty line marks end of headers
 IF line == "" THEN
 bodyStart = i + 1
 BREAK
 END IF
 
 // Handle header folding (multiline headers)
 IF line starts with " " OR line starts with "\t" THEN
 // Continuation of previous header
 previousHeader = GetLastKey(headers)
 headers[previousHeader] += " " + Trim(line)
 ELSE
 // New header
 colonPos = Find(line, ":")
 IF colonPos > 0 THEN
 headerName = Substring(line, 0, colonPos)
 headerValue = Substring(line, colonPos + 1)
 headers[headerName] = Trim(headerValue)
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Extract commonly needed headers
 result = {
 "from": headers["From"] OR headers["FROM"],
 "to": headers["To"] OR headers["TO"],
 "cc": headers["CC"] OR headers["cc"],
 "subject": headers["Subject"] OR headers["SUBJECT"],
 "date": headers["Date"] OR headers["DATE"],
 "messageId": headers["Message-ID"] OR headers["Message-Id"],
 "received": headers["Received"], // May have multiple
 "replyTo": headers["Reply-To"] OR headers["Reply-To"]
 }
 
 // Extract body
 result["body"] = Join(lines[bodyStart:], "\r\n")
 
 RETURN result
END
examples.explanation: This code parses email headers following RFC 822 format. It handles header folding (long headers split across lines), case-insensitive header names, and extracts essential fields like From, To, CC, Subject, Date, Message-ID, and body content.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is email content length
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is number of headers

examples.title: MIME Base64 Encoding
examples.description: Pseudocode for encoding binary data to Base64 for email transmission
examples.code:
PROCEDURE Base64Encode(binaryData)
BEGIN
 // Base64 alphabet
 alphabet = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789+/"
 
 // Convert binary to bit stream
 bits = ""
 FOR each byte IN binaryData DO
 bits += BinaryToString(byte, 8 bits)
 END FOR
 
 // Pad with zeros to make multiple of 6
 paddingNeeded = (6 - (bits.length MOD 6)) MOD 6
 bits += "0" * paddingNeeded
 
 // Convert to Base64 characters
 result = ""
 FOR i FROM 0 TO bits.length STEP 6 DO
 sixBits = Substring(bits, i, 6)
 index = BinaryToInt(sixBits)
 result += alphabet[index]
 END FOR
 
 // Add padding if needed
 IF paddingNeeded == 2 THEN
 result = Substring(result, 0, result.length - 2) + "=="
 ELSE IF paddingNeeded == 4 THEN
 result = Substring(result, 0, result.length - 1) + "="
 END IF
 
 RETURN result
END

PROCEDURE CreateMimeMessage(textContent, attachmentData, attachmentName)
BEGIN
 boundary = GenerateRandomString(32) // e.g., "----=_Part_1234567890"
 
 message = "MIME-Version: 1.0\r\n"
 message += "Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary=\"" + boundary + "\"\r\n"
 message += "\r\n"
 
 // Text part
 message += "--" + boundary + "\r\n"
 message += "Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8\r\n"
 message += "Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit\r\n"
 message += "\r\n"
 message += textContent + "\r\n"
 
 // Attachment part
 message += "--" + boundary + "\r\n"
 message += "Content-Type: application/octet-stream\r\n"
 message += "Content-Transfer-Encoding: base64\r\n"
 message += "Content-Disposition: attachment; filename=\"" + attachmentName + "\"\r\n"
 message += "\r\n"
 message += Base64Encode(attachmentData) + "\r\n"
 
 // End boundary
 message += "--" + boundary + "--\r\n"
 
 RETURN message
END
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates MIME encoding: Base64 encoding converts binary data (images, documents) into ASCII characters for safe email transmission. The CreateMimeMessage function builds a multipart MIME message with text content and binary attachments, using boundaries to separate parts.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for Base64 encoding where n is input size

examples.title: IMAP Email Retrieval
examples.description: Pseudocode showing IMAP protocol for retrieving emails from server
examples.code:
PROCEDURE RetrieveEmailsIMAP(server, username, password)
BEGIN
 // Connect to IMAP server
 socket = ConnectToServer(server, port 143)
 
 // Wait for server greeting
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Login
 tag = GenerateTag() // e.g., "A001"
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " LOGIN " + username + " " + password)
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 IF NOT response.startsWith(tag + " OK") THEN
 RETURN ERROR "Login failed"
 END IF
 
 // Select INBOX folder
 tag = GenerateTag()
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " SELECT INBOX")
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Get email count and UID validity
 emailCount = ExtractFromResponse(response, "EXISTS")
 
 // Fetch email headers (example: first email)
 IF emailCount > 0 THEN
 tag = GenerateTag()
 // Fetch envelope (headers) for message 1
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " FETCH 1 (ENVELOPE)")
 response = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Fetch email body
 tag = GenerateTag()
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " FETCH 1 (BODY[TEXT])")
 bodyResponse = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Mark email as seen (optional)
 tag = GenerateTag()
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " STORE 1 +FLAGS (\\Seen)")
 END IF
 
 // List all folders
 tag = GenerateTag()
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " LIST " * "INBOX")
 foldersResponse = ReceiveResponse(socket)
 
 // Logout
 tag = GenerateTag()
 SendCommand(socket, tag + " LOGOUT")
 CloseSocket(socket)
 
 RETURN emails
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows IMAP operations: connecting to port 143, logging in, selecting the INBOX folder, checking email count, fetching specific emails using FETCH command with various data items (ENVELOPE, BODY[TEXT]), modifying flags (marking as Seen), listing folders, and proper logout. IMAP keeps emails on server unlike POP3.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of emails
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing retrieved email data
